refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency compatible with safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR is already focused on a single obligation: ensuring teleoperation latency is compatible with safe remote operation. It does not combine multiple requirements or specify implementation details, thus it is atomic and solution-neutral.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure the end-to-end communication latency during teleoperation under agreed network conditions and verify that the 95th percentile latency (p95) is less than or equal to 150 ms, ensuring compliance with safety requirements.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency compatible with safe remote operation.

candidate_dps: Implement a dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation
Design a real-time data compression and prioritization mechanism to minimize transmission delays
Establish a distributed control architecture with local autonomy to reduce dependency on end-to-end communication
Incorporate predictive control algorithms to compensate for communication latency
Use a modular communication interface layer to abstract network variations and enable latency management
selected_dp.selected_dp: Implement a dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation

potential_sources: Tight coupling between communication protocol and control algorithms
Dependency on specific network conditions or hardware
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP focuses on the communication protocol layer, which can be designed independently from control algorithms and hardware implementations, supporting modularity and independence.

assumptions_risks: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters to meet latency targets
Protocol optimization alone can achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms latency without excessive complexity
Operational policies do not impose additional constraints that increase latency
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Consider explicitly including adaptive mechanisms in the DP to handle variable network conditions and ensure latency targets are met under diverse scenarios.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Module responsible for enabling remote control and communication with the vehicle, including communication protocol handling for teleoperation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bi-directional data flow for vehicle status and control feedback

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands and control instructions from remote operator

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical and logical network interface for communication

module_containment_scores.notes: Very broad module; contains many unrelated functions, leading to high diffusion and low cohesion for this DP.
Vehicle module focuses on physical vehicle systems; communication protocol for teleoperation is tangential and would cause diffusion.
Highly cohesive with the DP; communication protocol stack is central to teleoperation functionality, minimal diffusion expected.
Safety Services focus on safety alerts and monitoring; communication protocol for teleoperation is outside its scope, causing high diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most specific and cohesive boundary for implementing the dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation, as it directly manages remote control and communication functions.
containment_risks: Potential overlap with Network Link interface responsibilities if physical layer details are not clearly separated.
If teleoperation communication protocols evolve to require integration with Safety Services, cross-module coordination may increase.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly addresses the FR by specifying a dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation, which is necessary to achieve the required end-to-end latency compatible with safe remote operation (p95 ≤ 150 ms). This mapping is clear and focused on the functional requirement.

diffusion_risks: Potential overlap with Network Link interface responsibilities if physical layer details are not clearly separated.
If teleoperation communication protocols evolve to require integration with Safety Services, cross-module coordination may increase.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The Teleoperation module has the highest containment score (9) and lowest diffusion score (2) for this DP, indicating strong cohesion and minimal diffusion risk. It is the most appropriate IM boundary to implement the dedicated communication protocol stack. However, care must be taken to clearly separate physical network link responsibilities to avoid diffusion and to monitor evolving integration needs with Safety Services.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of the FR through the IM is feasible by testing the Teleoperation module's communication latency under agreed network conditions to ensure p95 ≤ 150 ms, and by validating compliance with worksite safety plans and operational policies.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of the DP to the Teleoperation IM is clear and well-justified, with explicit interfaces defined for Telemetry, Tasking, and Network Link, supporting traceability from FR to IM.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define and document the separation of responsibilities between the Teleoperation module and the Network Link interface to minimize diffusion risk.
Establish coordination protocols or interfaces between Teleoperation and Safety Services modules to manage potential future integration needs.
Include verification steps in the Teleoperation module development plan to measure and ensure latency requirements are met under realistic network conditions.

decision.justification: All gates (FR->DP, DP->IM, FR->IM) have passed with clear, focused mappings and strong containment in the Teleoperation module. Verification feasibility and allocation clarity are established, and recommended revisions address diffusion risks and integration coordination without requiring further decomposition. Given the high safety criticality, the current level sufficiently captures the necessary functional requirements and their implementation boundaries to proceed safely.

revise.why: To explicitly define and document the separation of responsibilities between the Teleoperation module and the Network Link interface, and to establish coordination protocols with Safety Services modules to manage potential integration needs.

assumptions: The Teleoperation module can maintain low diffusion risk with proper interface definitions.
Verification under realistic network conditions will confirm latency requirements are met.
open_risks: Potential overlap with Network Link interface if physical layer responsibilities are not clearly separated.
Evolving integration needs with Safety Services may increase cross-module coordination complexity.
evidence_needed: Documentation of responsibility separation between Teleoperation and Network Link interfaces.
Verification test results demonstrating p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under realistic network conditions.
Defined coordination protocols between Teleoperation and Safety Services modules.
